title: Porridge
subtitle: Scottish oat porridge with milk and a pinch of salt.
excerpt: Porridge oats simmered slowly in whole milk until thick and creamy. Finished with a pinch of salt. Serve with honey, jam, or fruit. The simplest and most sustaining breakfast there is.
sourceNotes: Porridge has been eaten in Scotland and northern England for centuries, made from oats that thrived in the cool, wet climate. The traditional version was made with water and salt alone; milk became standard as it grew cheaper and more accessible. Samuel Johnson's 1755 dictionary definition reads: 'oats, a grain which in England is generally given to horses, but in Scotland supports the people'. It reflects the dish's long association with Scottish diet.

content.text: Good porridge needs only two things: slow heat and patience. Rushed over a high flame, the oats turn gluey before the centre of each grain has softened. Cooked slowly over medium-low heat with regular stirring, the oats swell evenly and the porridge becomes thick but still pourable.
Use whole milk for the creamiest result. Water works but produces a thinner, plainer porridge. A half-and-half split of milk and water is the practical middle ground for every day.
A medium saucepan and a wooden spoon.

Put {{porridge-oats}} of oats in a medium saucepan with {{whole-milk}} of milk and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to a gentle simmer over a medium heat, stirring regularly. Once simmering, reduce the heat to medium-low and cook for 5 to 6 minutes, stirring frequently, until thick and creamy. The porridge should coat the back of a spoon but still pour.

Spoon into warm bowls. Drizzle with honey, or serve with jam, fresh fruit, or a splash of cold milk poured around the edge. The contrast of cold milk against hot porridge is the traditional Scottish finish.

items.fix: Stir continuously and reduce the heat. A heavy-based pan also helps prevent sticking.
items.cause: Heat too high or not stirred often enough.
items.symptom: Porridge is sticking to the pan

items.fix: Add a splash of warm milk and stir until the right consistency returns. A little cold milk also works.
items.symptom: Porridge is too thick and solid

items.fix: Cook for a few more minutes over a lower heat, stirring constantly, until it thickens. It will thicken further as it cools in the bowl.
items.cause: Too much liquid for the oat quantity.

items.fix: Salt is not optional in porridge. It brings out the oat flavour. Add more and taste. Adjust the milk ratio towards less water next time.
items.cause: Not enough salt, or too little milk relative to oats.

content.text: Porridge is the morning meal of Scotland in a way that no other dish belongs to any single country in British cooking. It crossed into England and Wales slowly, carried by Scottish migration and the rising health reputation of oats from the nineteenth century onwards. The World Porridge Making Championship, held annually in Carrbridge in the Scottish Highlands, has run since 1994. Arguments about the right consistency, the right toppings, and whether sugar or salt should finish it are unresolved and likely to stay that way.
